title: Suguru (Dvādaśāvarta) Vāndaṇā Sūtra — Twelve-Fold Salutation to the Guru
summary: This is the prayer by which a practitioner formally bows to and venerates their guru (spiritual teacher). It is not just words: it is recited while performing a fixed set of reverential gestures and bows, asking the guru's permission to approach, inquiring after the guru's well-being, and finally begging forgiveness for any disrespect shown during the day. It is called 'twelve-fold' (dvādaśāvarta) because of the twelve ritual hand-turnings (āvarta) made while reciting it. Because a Jain treats reverence for the enlightened teacher as essential to spiritual progress, this salutation is one of the six daily obligations.
ritualPlacement: This sūtra is the anchor of the Third Āvaśyaka — Vandanaa (Vandanaka, 'veneration of the guru') — one of the six fixed daily obligations. In the base Devasi (daily, sunset) pratikramaṇa it is recited after the Paḍilehaṇa (50-bol inspection) and before the Laghu Atiyār (the short confession of vow-transgressions). Because the core Āvaśyaka sūtras are shared, the very same text is reused in Rāi, Pakkhī, Cāumāsī and Sāṁvatsarī. Only one thing changes by occasion: the period-word. Where the daily form says 'devasiyaṁ / devasiaṁ / devasio' (vv. 6–7, 'of the day'), the fortnightly form substitutes 'pakkhiyaṁ', the seasonal 'cāumāsiyaṁ', and the annual 'saṁvacchariyaṁ'. Cāumāsī and Sāṁvatsarī have no independent sūtra text of their own — they are Pakkhī with this word-substitution. The whole salutation is performed as a paired cycle (recited twice), which is what produces the twelve āvartas.
authorAndSource: Anonymous canonical liturgy in Ardhamāgadhī Prakrit; not ascribed to a named author. The Vandanaka is part of the ancient six-fold Āvaśyaka corpus expounded in the Āvaśyaka-sūtra and its Niryukti/Bhāṣya tradition; the text and choreography belong to the shared Śvetāmbara heritage. The reading and ritual placement followed here are those printed verbatim in the Anchalgachchha (Vidhipaksha) edition 'Panch Pratikraman Sutra — Vidhi Sahit', the golden source for this corpus.
structureArc: The seven units trace a single act of approach-and-homage, done twice. (1) The opening request — 'Icchāmi khamāsamaṇo! vandiuṁ…' — asks leave to bow, 'to the limit of my capacity' (jāvaṇijjāe) and 'with cessation of sinful activity' (nisīhiāe). (2) 'Aṇujāṇaha me miuggahaṁ' seeks the guru's permission to enter the avagraha, the small consecrated space around the guru. (3) The central unit 'nisīhi — a, ho, kāyaṁ, kāya saṁphāsaṁ…' carries the āvarta gestures and the well-being inquiry: 'May the strain be forgivable to you; has your day passed in great comfort, with little fatigue?' (4) 'Jattā bhe?' asks after the guru's spiritual restraint/endeavour (yātrā). (5) 'Javaṇijjaṁ ca bhe?' asks whether the guru's body and senses are sustaining without affliction (yāpanīya). (6) 'Khāmemi khamāsamaṇo! devasiyaṁ vaikkamaṁ' begs forgiveness for the day's transgression. (7) The closing 'Āvassiāe paḍikkamāmi…' is a full pratikramaṇa of disrespect to the guru: confessing any one of the thirty-three āśātanās (tittīsannayarāe), faults of mind, speech and body, and the four passions (anger, pride, deceit, greed), and ending 'paḍikkamāmi, nindāmi, garihāmi, appāṇaṁ vosirāmi' — I turn back, I censure, I reproach myself, I renounce the misdeed.
recurringImagery: The sustained motif of this sūtra is gesture and dialogue rather than verbal metaphor: it is a choreographed, two-party exchange between disciple and guru. Read it as an embodied ritual in which speech, posture and movement coincide. Tradition counts the act as twenty-five 'essentials' (āvaśyaka-sthāna) performed across the paired recitation — classically enumerated as 2 avanata (bowings), 1 yathājāta (the 'as-newborn', humble folded posture), 12 āvarta (the turning movements that give the sūtra its name 'dvādaśāvarta'), 4 śiraḥ (head-obeisances), 3 gupti (guard of mind, speech and body), and the entering and leaving of the guru's avagraha. The well-being questions of vv. 3–5 (comfort, fatigue, spiritual restraint, bodily sustenance) form a single recurring register of solicitous inquiry; treat them as one connected courtesy, not three separate topics. Each āvarta is a reverential turning of the joined hands from a gesture toward the guru back to one's own forehead — the physical enactment of the homage the words declare.
keyThemes: Veneration of the guru as a daily obligation (Third Āvaśyaka)
Embodied ritual: speech united with gesture, bow and posture
Seeking permission to approach the guru's consecrated space (avagraha)
Solicitous inquiry into the guru's comfort, restraint and bodily sustenance
Confession of disrespect (āśātanā) and the four passions toward the guru
Pratikramaṇa and self-renunciation: nindāmi, garihāmi, appāṇaṁ vosirāmi
A shared sūtra reused across all five pratikramaṇas via period-word substitution

glossary.definition: Reverential salutation/veneration; the Third of the six daily obligations (Āvaśyaka), directed to the guru.
'Twelve turnings' — the twelve ritual hand-movements (āvarta) made across the paired recitation, which name this salutation.
A reverential turning gesture of the joined hands, moving from the guru back to one's own forehead, made at fixed words.
'Forgiveness-ascetic' — an honorific for the guru/monk, addressed in vocative as khamāsamaṇo.
An obligatory daily duty; here also the 25 'essentials' (sthāna) said to constitute a correct vandana.
The small consecrated space immediately around the guru; the disciple asks permission (aṇujāṇaha) before entering it.
Declared cessation/abandonment of sinful activity, undertaken on entering the act of homage (nisīhiāe).
Sustainable capacity; used both for bowing 'to the limit of my capacity' and for asking whether the guru's body and senses are sustaining without affliction.
Spiritual 'journey' — the guru's continued restraint and ascetic endeavour, inquired after in 'jattā bhe?'.
Disrespect or slight toward the guru; the closing verse confesses any one of the traditional thirty-three āśātanās.
The four passions — anger, pride, deceit and greed — confessed as causes of fault toward the guru.
'Turning back' — the act of reflective confession and atonement; here applied specifically to disrespect of the guru.
'May the misdeed be void/vain' — the formula renouncing a wrongful act.
Renunciation/casting-off; 'appāṇaṁ vosirāmi' — I abandon the (sinful) self/act, the closing surrender.

howToRead: A newcomer should read this as a respectful, slightly formal visit to one's teacher — asking to come close, inquiring after their comfort, and apologizing for any rudeness; do not worry about the gestures on a first reading, just follow the courtesy of the words. An advanced practitioner should read each phrase against its prescribed posture and the count of 25 āvaśyaka-sthānas (the 12 āvartas, the bows and head-obeisances), and note the period-word that marks which pratikramaṇa is in progress.

sources.title: Panch Pratikraman Sutra — Vidhi Sahit (Anchalgachchha / Vidhipaksha edition)
sources.consultedFor: Verbatim sūtra text, the Anchalgachchha reading, and ritual placement within the Devasi/Pakkhī flow

sources.title: Āvaśyaka-sūtra and Niryukti tradition (Śvetāmbara Āgama)
sources.consultedFor: Identity of Vandanaka as the Third Āvaśyaka and the structure of the dvādaśāvarta vandana (25 sthānas, 12 āvartas, 33 āśātanās)

sources.title: H. D. T. Sheth, Pāia-sadda-mahaṇṇavo (Prakrit–Sanskrit Dictionary)
sources.consultedFor: Glosses of Prakrit terms: jāvaṇijja, nisīhiā, miuggaha, jattā, āsāyaṇā, vosirāmi

notes: The seven 'verses' are ritual units, not metrical gāthās; their division and the bracketed/spaced syllables (e.g. 'a — ho, kā — yaṁ') in the source mark where the āvarta gestures fall and are preserved as printed. The enumeration of 25 āvaśyaka-sthānas (2 avanata + 1 yathājāta + 12 āvarta + 4 śiraḥ + 3 gupti + 2 praveśa + 1 niṣkramaṇa) and the list of 33 āśātanās belong to the broader Śvetāmbara Āvaśyaka commentary tradition; the source book prints the sūtra text and vidhi but the per-occasion reader should confirm any sthāna-count against the book's own vidhi rather than treat the standard enumeration as the book's wording. The closing verse's period-word (devasiya → pakkhiya → cāumāsiya → saṁvacchariya) is the only substitution across the five pratikramaṇas. Web verification returned only generic material; the doctrinal claims above rest on the tradition knowledge base and standard Āvaśyaka scholarship — treat specific page-level citations as not independently web-verified.
